other_observers: Barbara Byron was with me on 6/1/10 and the later dates. Peter Derven, Ellen Derven, Jim Beatty joined us on 6/3/10.; Yes, I am the original finder and identifier of the bird. No one was allowed to see the bird independently because of private property concerns.
duration: About 7 mins. on 6/3/2010 (between 8:15 AM and 9:45 AM)
description: For size of the bird, I'd estimate that the bird appeared to be slightly larger than a junco, but smaller than a Hermit Thrush. The shape appeared somewhat like a thrush. The bird had an orange crown-patch with wide, lateral crown-stripes of a dark brown (almost black) color. The face was a light, brown color. A wide, white eye-ring was evident. The white throat was set off by malar streaks. The white throat also contrasted with the heavily marked chest. At a distance those markings on the chest appeared as very wide, dark streaks. The belly was white. The upperparts and wings appeared brown in color. There were no wing bars. The bill was very light-colored from underneath.Behaviors: The bird sang from fairly high perches on his territory. The bird was extremely difficult to find, particularly in this habitat. The habitat was ponderosa forest that provided significant canopy cover.Call: The bird was initially identified by the song. The song was composed of 4 notes, a slight pause, followed by 4 more notes. All notes were on the same pitch. The 2 sets of 4 notes were all at an even rhythm. Each note rang out.Plumage: Alternate
